nativeRange.habitat: Small blackwater streams and swamps in the Madre de Dios River basin; slow-moving or still water over leaf litter and sandy substrate with dense riparian vegetation and overhanging roots

decorPreferences: numerous clay or coconut-shell caves for spawning
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to generate tannins and blackwater conditions
driftwood and bogwood for cover and pH buffering
dense planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, Vallisneria) to break lines of sight between females
peat filtration or blackwater extract to achieve target pH and soft water

diet.notes: In the wild, this species feeds primarily on small invertebrates and insect matter. In the aquarium, it tends to thrive on a varied diet of live and frozen foods — Artemia (brine shrimp), Cyclops, Daphnia, bloodworms, micro worms, and other small invertebrates. High-quality micro-pellets and fine granules may be accepted, but live and frozen foods are strongly preferred and are generally considered essential for bringing breeders into condition. Offering multiple small portions throughout the day is advisable, as uneaten food breaks down quickly and can degrade the soft, acidic water chemistry this species requires.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetra, ember tetra, rummy-nose tetra) sharing blackwater parameters
small Corydoras (sterbai, habrosus) adapted to warm soft water
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina) may be tolerated in well-planted tanks but fry risk exists
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species or males of the same species in small tanks
boisterous or fast-moving mid-water fish that compete for food
hard-water cichlids incompatible with acidic parameters

imageCandidates.notes: Species type specimen image on FishBase summary page; verify license before use.

summary: A rare Peruvian blackwater dwarf cichlid from the Madre de Dios basin, named after co-expedition leader Jorge Andrés Urteaga Cavero. Males reach ~4 cm and display subtle metallic teal coloration; females turn vivid yellow during brood care. A haremic cave-spawner requiring soft, acidic water and expert-level water management.
careNotes: The Urteaga Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma urteagai) is a blackwater species from the Madre de Dios River basin in southeastern Peru. Males reach around 4.1 cm in length, making it one of the smallest members of the genus, and the species is considered rare in the aquarium trade — it is maintained primarily by specialist dwarf-cichlid keepers working with wild-collected or first-generation captive-bred (F1) stock. Strict water chemistry is essential: pH 4.5–6.5, GH 0–8, KH 0–4. Peat filtration, Indian almond leaves, alder cones, or commercial blackwater extract should be used to achieve and maintain the required soft, acidic conditions. Even brief exposure to hard or alkaline water is reported to stress the fish and suppress breeding activity. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a harem group consisting of one male and two to three females; 80–100 L is strongly preferred to allow each female to establish her own territory around a spawning cave. Fine sand substrate is recommended to support the species' natural sifting behavior. The tank should include an abundance of caves — clay pots, coconut shells, or ceramic tubes — along with dense planting and driftwood to break sightlines between females, which will defend their clutches from conspecifics and other intruders. Any tankmates should be small, non-competitive species that are comfortable in blackwater conditions; cardinal tetras, pencilfish, and Otocinclus are the companions most commonly recommended. Males display subdued metallic teal coloration along the mid-body when courting; females transform to a vivid yellow with bold black markings when guarding eggs or fry. The species belongs to the A. regani group within the resticulosa complex. Bringing breeders into condition requires consistent live and frozen foods and consistently clean, soft, acidic water.
breedingNotes: The Urteaga Dwarf Cichlid is a haremic cave-spawner: a single dominant male courts and spawns with multiple females, each of which claims her own territory around a separate spawning site. Females deposit eggs on the ceiling of a cave — an inverted clay pot or coconut-shell hide works well — and provide exclusive brood care; the male plays no direct role in protecting the fry. Clutches tend to be modest in size for an Apistogramma. Eggs generally hatch within 48–72 hours at 26–28°C, and the fry are typically free-swimming within 5–7 days. The female guards and fans the fry actively for approximately four weeks after they become free-swimming. First foods for the fry should be freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms. The species is reported by hobbyist breeders to be unusually prolific and attentive as a parent relative to other Apistogramma, with breeding groups documented producing 150–200 juveniles under good conditions. Soft, acidic water in the pH 5.0–6.0 range with near-zero hardness is reported to improve hatch rates and favor more balanced sex ratios. If female-to-female aggression escalates within the group, removing or partitioning the male is generally advisable.

primary.name: FishBase — Apistogramma urteagai
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biometric reference. Confirmed adult size (max 4.1 cm SL males), order Cichliformes, family Cichlidae, native range (Madre de Dios River basin, Peru, Amazon drainage), water parameters (pH 5.0–6.0, dH 6–10, temperature 22–29°C), habitat (small streams and swamps), and reproductive note (eggs deposited on cave ceiling, tended by female).

additional.name: PVAS — Apistogramma urteagai: Collecting, Keeping and Breeding a Wild Apisto
additional.notes: Detailed first-person hobbyist account of wild-collected specimens. Confirmed haremic polygamous behavior (1 male, multiple females), ease of breeding when water conditions met, prolific nature (150–200 juveniles across breeding group), use of clay pots and driftwood, fry raised on brine shrimp nauplii, and blackwater/acidic water requirements. Primary behavioral and breeding reference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlids
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guidance used to cross-check tank size (20 gal / ~75 L minimum for a pair or small group), cave-spawning behavior, blackwater setup with Indian almond leaves and tannins, fine sand substrate, and compatible tankmates. No dedicated A. urteagai page; general guidance applied to species.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Apistogramma urteagai
additional.notes: Wholesale/trade note confirming Madre de Dios, Peru origin, undemanding relative to many Apistogramma, and polygamous harem behavior. Noted as offered only occasionally in the trade. Cross-referenced coloration: male metallic teal mid-body, females vivid yellow during brood care.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Apistogramma urteagai
additional.notes: Independent hobbyist reference confirming blackwater/acidic requirement (peat filtration recommended), polygamous harem ratio (3 females to 1 male), cave spawning, female-only brood care for ~4 weeks post free-swimming, and species rarity in trade. Water parameters (pH 5.0–6.5, GH 0–8, KH 0–4) cross-checked here.

fish.breedingNotes: A cave-spawner in which the female deposits eggs on the ceiling of a spawning cave and provides all brood care for approximately four weeks after the fry become free-swimming. Soft, acidic blackwater conditions — pH 5.0–6.0 with near-zero hardness — are generally considered necessary for reliable spawning and healthy sex ratios. A haremic setup with one male and two to three females, each with her own cave territory, is the recommended approach. The species tends to be prolific once water conditions are met; fry accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii as a first food.
